content.full_text:
OFF 24 '91 15:14 BERKELEY OFF "UP. 415 644-6035                                    F.2

                              CITY OF BERKELEY
                                                                      Carla Woodworth
                                                                      COUNCILMEMBER
                                                                      DISTRICT 7

                                                                      April 21, 1991

Jonathan Rothman
Legal Counsel
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street, Suite 800
Sacramento, CA 95814
ARTS FAX: 497-2026

Dear Mr. Rothman:

     On Monday, April 22, 1991, I received telephone advice from
both the on-duty advice attorney and you regarding my possible
conflict of interest on a matter pending before the Berkeley City
Council.

     On your advice, which was based on a cautious preliminary
evaluation of the facts, I recused myself from participation in
the matter. Unfortunately, the City Council was deadlocked and
unable to take any action.

     The purpose of this letter is to request a more detailed
evaluation of my situation. As you will see from the description
that follows, there is some urgency to this request as it
involves the possible insolvency of one of our municipal
agencies, as early as April 27, 1991.

BACKGROUND

     The Berkeley Rent Stabilization Program (hereinafter "Rent
Board") is an independent branch of city government with its own
elected Board. The Rent Board collects fees to support its
activities.

     One of the Rent Board's authorities is the adjustment of
individual rents through a landlord-initiated petition and
hearing process. My landlord has petitioned for a rent increase
and the hearing has been scheduled for hearing on May 9, 1991.
(The Board's preliminary analysis of the landlord's petition
estimates a $210 per month rent increase for my apartment if so
provided.)

     The Rent Board is running out of money. City Manager
has projected that the Board will be out of money by April 27,
1991. In order to meet its operating expenses for the fiscal
year ending June 30, 1991, the Rent Board has requested a loan
from the City Council of $200,000.

_______________________________________________________________________________
     Martin Luther King Jr. Civic Center    2180 Milvia Street      Berkeley, California 94704

                                    Telephone (415) 644-6298
                                    Facsimile (415) 644-6035

OFF 24 '91 15:14                                                             PAGE.002

RFF 24 '91 15:15 BERKELEY CITY MGR. JUS EDUC05E                                P.3


Jonathan Rottman
April 24, 1991
Page 2

     The overlap of my hearing date and the period of the Rent
Board's possible insolvency is what prompted my inquiry. If I
were to vote against the loan, it might appear that I was trying
to shut the Rent Board down in order to postpone my hearing.
(Ironically, my recusal on the subject could have the same
result.)

     I should point out that the debate over the loan was not
between those in favor or those opposed. Rather, it was over the
types of conditions that should be attached to the loan. The
failure of the City Council to resolve this question prevented
the loan from being arrowed. If I am allowed to participate,
the City Council could call a special meeting to resolve the loan
issue. In the absence of such participation the future of the
Ren Board is uncertain.

     I would appreciate a prompt review of my situation and a
telephone report of your conclusions as soon as possible.

     Thank you for the came courteous and thoughtful treatment
that characterized your tenure with our city's legal department.

                                                Sincerely,

                                                [signature]
                                                Carla Woodworth
                                                City Councilmember


cc:  City Manager
     City Attorney




RFF 24 '91 15:43                                             PAGE.003

sections.facts:
The Berkeley Rent Stabilization Program (hereinafter "Rent Board") is an independent branch of city government with its own elected Board. The Rent Board collects fees to support its activities.

One of the Rent Board's activities is the adjustment of individual rents through a landlord-initiated petition and hearing process. My landlord has petitioned for a rent increase and the matter has been scheduled for hearing on May 9, 1991. (The Board's preliminary analysis of the landlord's petition estimates a $230 per month rent increase for my apartment if he prevails.)

The Rent Board is running out of money. Our City Manager has projected that the Board will be out of money by April 27, 1991. In order to meet its operating expenses for the fiscal year ending June 30, 1991, the Rent Board has requested a loan from the City Council of $200,000.

Jonathan Rotnman  
April 24, 1991

The overlap of my hearing date and the period of the Rent Board's possible insolvency is what prompted my inquiry. If I were to vote against the loan, it might appear that I was trying to shut the Rent Board down in order to postpone my hearing. (Ironically, my recusal on the subject could have the same result.)

I should point out that the debate over the loan was not between those in favor or those opposed. Rather, it was over the types of conditions that should be attached to the loan. The failure of the City Council to resolve this question prevented the loan from being approved. If I am allowed to participate, the City Council could call a special meeting to resolve the loan issue. In the absence of such participation the future of the Rent Board is uncertain.

I would appreciate a prompt review of my situation and a telephone report of your conclusions as soon as possible.

Thank you for the same courteous and thoughtful treatment that characterized your tenure with our city's legal department.
